title: Μῦς ἀρουραῖος καὶ μῦς ἀστικός
titleEn: The Country Mouse and the Town Mouse
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 243 (= Perry 352). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« », ⟨ ⟩ for editorial supplements). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Μῦς ἀρουραῖος τὸν ἐν οἴκῳ ἐφίλει.
sentences.tr: Mys arouraios ton en oikōi ephilei.
sentences.l: A mouse of-the-fields the one in the-house held-dear.
sentences.i: A country mouse was friends with a house mouse.
sentences.n: The first verse fable in the collection: Chambry's chosen text here is Byzantine twelve-syllable verse, one line per breath. τὸν ἐν οἴκῳ — the article plus a prepositional phrase makes a noun: 'the (one) in the house.'

sentences.t:
Ὁ δὲ τοῦ οἴκου κληθεὶς ὑπὸ τοῦ φίλου
ἦλθεν εὐθέως δειπνήσων εἰς ἀρούρας.
sentences.tr:
Ho de tou oikou klētheis hypo tou philou
ēlthen eutheōs deipnēsōn eis arouras.
sentences.l:
And the one of-the house, invited by the friend,
came straightway, about-to-dine, to the fields.
sentences.i: The house mouse, invited by his friend, came straight out to the fields to dine.
sentences.n: δειπνήσων — a future participle of purpose: 'in order to dine.' The same device as ὡς ἀφαιρησομένη in the Dog fable, here without ὡς.

sentences.t: Ὁ δὲ ἐσθίων κριθὰς καὶ σῖτον ἔφη·
sentences.tr: Ho de esthiōn krithas kai siton ephē:
sentences.l: And he, eating barley-corns and grain, said:
sentences.i: As he ate his barley and grain, he said:

sentences.t:
« Γίνωσκε, φίλε, μυρμήκων ζῇς τὸν βίον·
ἐπείπερ δ' ἐμοὶ ἀγαθῶν ἐστι πλῆθος,
ἐμοὶ σύνελθε καὶ ἀπολαύσεις πάντων. »
sentences.tr:
« Ginōske, phile, myrmēkōn zēis ton bion:
epeiper d' emoi agathōn esti plēthos,
emoi synelthe kai apolauseis pantōn. »
sentences.i: « Face it, my friend: you live the life of an ant. But since I have good things in plenty, come along with me and you shall enjoy them all. »
sentences.n: μυρμήκων ζῇς τὸν βίον, 'you live the life of ants' — remember the ants' actual life from the Cicada fable: endless food-gathering. ἐμοί … ἐστι, the dative of possession: 'there is to me' = 'I have.'

sentences.i: And off the two of them went at once.

sentences.t:
Καὶ ὃς ὑπεδείκνυ ὄσπρια καὶ σῖτον,
φοίνικας ἅμα, τυρόν, μέλι, ὀπώρας.
sentences.tr:
Kai hos hypedeiknu ospria kai siton,
phoinikas hama, tyron, meli, opōras.
sentences.l:
And he was-showing pulses and grain,
dates besides, cheese, honey, fruits.
sentences.i: And he showed him beans and grain, dates too, cheese, honey, fruit.
sentences.n: καὶ ὅς, 'and he' — the relative pronoun in its ancient demonstrative sense, surviving in narrative formulas. The larder is a bare list in the accusative, one delicacy per foot.

sentences.t:
Ὁ δ' αὖ θαυμάζων αὐτὸν ηὐλόγει σφόδρα
καὶ τὴν ἑαυτοῦ κατεμέμφετο τύχην.
sentences.tr:
Ho d' au thaumazōn auton ēulogei sphodra
kai tēn heautou katemempheto tychēn.
sentences.l:
And he in-turn, marveling, was-blessing him greatly
and the of-himself was-blaming fortune.
sentences.i: The other, marveling, praised him warmly — and kept cursing his own lot.

sentences.t:
Βουλομένων δὲ ἀπάρξασθαι ἐσθίειν,
ἤνοιξεν εὐθὺς ἄνθρωπός τις τὴν θύραν.
sentences.tr:
Boulomenōn de aparxasthai esthiein,
ēnoixen euthys anthrōpos tis tēn thyran.
sentences.l:
And they wishing to-make-a-beginning of-eating,
opened at-once a-man certain the door.
sentences.i: But just as they were about to fall to, a man suddenly opened the door.
sentences.n: Βουλομένων … — a genitive absolute with the pronoun omitted: '(they) wishing to begin.' The door opens mid-verse.

sentences.t:
Φοβηθέντες δὲ οἱ δειλαῖοι τὸν κτύπον
εἰσεπήδησαν οἱ μῦς εἰς τὰς ῥαγάδας.
sentences.tr:
Phobēthentes de hoi deilaioi ton ktypon
eisepēdēsan hoi mys eis tas rhagadas.
sentences.l:
And frightened, the poor-wretches, at-the noise,
leapt the mice into the cracks.
sentences.i: Terrified by the noise, the poor mice leapt into the cracks.

sentences.t:
Ὡς δὲ ἤθελον πάλιν ἰσχάδας ἆραι,
ἧκεν ἕτερος τοῦ λαβεῖν τι τῶν ἔνδον.
sentences.tr:
Hōs de ēthelon palin ischadas arai,
hēken heteros tou labein ti tōn endon.
sentences.l:
And when they-wished again dried-figs to-take-up,
came another-man for-the taking of-something of-the things-inside.
sentences.i: When they crept out again to pick up some dried figs, in came another man to fetch something from the stores.
sentences.n: τοῦ λαβεῖν — the articular infinitive in the genitive expressing purpose: 'for the taking.' Another workhorse construction; watch for it in prose too.

sentences.t:
Οἱ δὲ καὶ πάλιν θεασάμενοι τοῦτον
εἰσεπήδησαν κρυβέντες ἐπὶ τρώγλης.
sentences.tr:
Hoi de kai palin theasamenoi touton
eisepēdēsan krybentes epi trōglēs.
sentences.l:
And they, once again having-caught-sight-of him,
leapt-in, hidden in a-hole.
sentences.i: The moment they saw him, they leapt away again and hid in a hole.

sentences.t:
Ὁ δ' ἀρουραῖος ὀλιγωρῶν τῇ πείνῃ
ἀνεστέναξε καὶ πρὸς τὸν ἄλλον ἔφη·
sentences.tr:
Ho d' arouraios oligōrōn tēi peinēi
anestenaxe kai pros ton allon ephē:
sentences.l:
And the field-mouse, making-light of-the hunger,
groaned-aloud and to the other said:
sentences.i: Then the country mouse, hunger notwithstanding, heaved a groan and said to his friend:

sentences.t:
« Χαῖρε σύ, φίλε, κατεσθίων εἰς κόρον,
ἐπαπολαύων αὐτὰ μετ' εὐφροσύνης
καὶ τοῦ κινδύνου καὶ τοῦ πολλοῦ τοῦ φόβου·
ἐγὼ δ' ὁ τάλας κριθὴν καὶ σῖτον τρώγων
ζήσω ἀφόβως μηδένα ὑποπτεύων. »
sentences.tr:
« Chaire sy, phile, katesthiōn eis koron,
epapolauōn auta met' euphrosynēs
kai tou kindynou kai tou pollou tou phobou:
egō d' ho talas krithēn kai siton trōgōn
zēsō aphobōs mēdena hypopteuōn. »
sentences.l:
« Farewell to-you, friend, devouring to satiety,
enjoying-on-top these-things with merriment —
both the danger and the much fear;
but I, the wretch, barley and grain nibbling,
shall-live fearlessly, no-one suspecting. »
sentences.i: « Goodbye then, friend: eat yourself sick, and relish it all — the danger and the great fear included. Poor wretch that I am, I'll gnaw my barley and grain and live without fear, suspecting nobody. »
sentences.n: Χαῖρε is both 'hello' and 'goodbye' — here the farewell. The sting is in the paired καί's: he 'enjoys' the feast together with (καὶ … καί) the danger and the fear — the menu and the terror served as one dish.

sentences.t:
Ὁ μῦθος δηλοῖ ὅτι
τὸ λιτῶς διάγειν καὶ ζῆν ἀταράχως
ὑπὲρ τὸ τρυφᾶν ἐν φόβῳ μετ' ὀδύνης.
sentences.tr:
Ho mythos dēloi hoti
to litōs diagein kai zēn atarachōs
hyper to tryphan en phobōi met' odynēs.
sentences.l:
The fable shows that
the plainly living and being untroubled
is-above the luxuriating in fear with anguish.
sentences.i: The fable shows that a plain and untroubled life is better than luxury enjoyed in fear and anguish.
sentences.n: Even the moral is in verse. Two articular infinitives are weighed against each other with ὑπέρ, 'above': τὸ λιτῶς διάγειν versus τὸ τρυφᾶν. ἀταράχως — 'untroubledly': ataraxia, the philosophers' word for peace of mind, in adverb form.
